FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data, N=10,054, 12-13 years, Year 3 (2019-2021)

FT_IC2.evidence: Exposure variables are R-rated movies and mature-rated video games consumption. Neither meets phone/SM criteria. Movies and games consumption are content ratings, not phone/SM-specific variables (E17 - video games unspecified platform applies). No smartphone use, time on phone, or social media variables measured.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis. Ordinal logistic regression models examining associations between media parenting practices (IV) and R-rated movies and mature-rated video game consumption (DV)

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in BMC Pediatrics (2025); not a duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Paper fails FT_IC2 (no phone/SM exposure variable), so FT_EC7 does not apply
notes: EXCLUDE: Paper focuses on media content ratings (R-rated movies, mature video games) rather than smartphone or social media use. This is a parenting study examining associations between parental media practices and content consumption, not smartphone/SM exposure.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data, N=9,519, ages 9-10 at baseline to 12-13 at Year 3 (2016-2021)

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific smartphone and digital media variables: TV shows/movies (0.99-1.95 h/day), video games (0.89-2.24 h/day), and digital socializing/texting/video chatting/social media (0.98-1.96 h/day). Screen time measured across TV, video games, texting, video chatting, browsing internet, and social media.

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports inferential statistical tests via compositional data analysis (CoDA) with isometric log-ratio transformations and mixed-effects modeling. Screen time linked to changes in sleep, sports/exercise activity, and other activities. Test statistics reported as change from baseline with 95% CI.

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports numeric values and confidence intervals: Screen time increased by 0.23 h/day from baseline to Year 1 (95% CI: 0.22, 0.24, p<0.001), 1.58 h/day from baseline to Year 2 (95% CI: 1.53, 1.63, p<0.001), and 3.28 h/day from baseline to Year 3 (95% CI: 3.10, 3.46, p<0.001)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis. Mixed-effects models with compositional data analysis examining four annual assessments of movement behaviors over time

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in BMC Pediatrics (2026); not a duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional age progression analysis with time-varying covariates; screen time is primary IV; sleep and sports/exercise are DVs. Age is a time-varying covariate (age effect modeled). Multiple outcomes examined beyond demographic prediction. Not purely longitudinal demographic-only prediction model.
notes: INCLUDE: Longitudinal study measuring specific smartphone and digital media use (screen time subtypes) with inferential statistics linking to non-SM outcomes (sleep, physical activity). Compositional data analysis appropriately handles 24-hour time-use paradigm.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data, Years 2, 3, and 4 follow-up data, N=2,749, mean age 11.9 at Year 2

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific gaming variables: daily gaming duration (Year 2) and problematic gaming via Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (VGAQ) (Year 2). Gaming is a smartphone/app-based activity.

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports causal mediation analysis with formal inferential testing. Examines gaming duration/problems (Year 2) → caffeine use (Year 3) → sleep duration/difficulties (Year 4). Reports path coefficients and statistical tests for indirect and direct effects.

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports numeric path coefficients with 95% confidence intervals. Example: Caffeine use significantly partially mediated relationship between problematic gaming and sleep difficulties (indirect effect = 0.023, 95% CI [0.004, 0.049]; direct effect = 0.036, 95% CI [0.016, 0.055]); problematic gaming and sleep duration (indirect effect = -0.006, 95% CI [-0.014, -0.001])

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis. Longitudinal mediation analysis using causal inference R package examining gaming duration and problematic gaming as exposures across multiple time points

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Journal of Behavioral Addictions (2025); not a duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Gaming is independent variable (IV) predicting sleep outcomes as dependent variables (DV). Caffeine serves as mediator. Individual-level behavioral predictor (gaming) involved, not demographic-only. Mediation analysis indicates complex analytical model beyond simple longitudinal demographic prediction.
notes: INCLUDE: Longitudinal mediation analysis with gaming (smartphone-based activity) as IV and sleep as DV. Reports extractable coefficients for gaming→sleep association mediated by caffeine use. Meets all inclusion criteria for FT-IC1 through FT-IC6.
